questions.question: What is the main security check in the E91 protocol?
questions.explanation: E91's key security feature is testing the CHSH Bell inequality. A violation (S > 2) proves the correlations are genuinely quantum and cannot have been predetermined by an eavesdropper, providing security verification beyond just checking QBER.

questions.question: What is the classical bound for the CHSH Bell inequality?
questions.explanation: The CHSH inequality states |S| ≤ 2 for any classical (local hidden variable) theory. Quantum mechanics can achieve up to S = 2√2 ≈ 2.83. Any measured value S > 2 proves quantum correlations.

questions.question: How does photon loss affect entanglement-based QKD compared to prepare-and-measure?

options.text: Loss has no effect on entanglement

questions.explanation: In entanglement-based QKD, BOTH photons must arrive for a successful measurement. If transmission is η, the coincidence rate scales as η². This quadratic penalty means entanglement-based QKD typically has shorter range than prepare-and-measure.

questions.question: What does 'device-independent' mean in DI-QKD?

options.text: Security doesn't depend on trusting the measurement devices

questions.explanation: Device-independent QKD means security is guaranteed even if the measurement devices are completely untrusted (potentially manufactured by an adversary). Security relies solely on the observed Bell violation and cannot be faked by any classical device.

questions.question: In BBM92, which Bell state gives Alice and Bob the same measurement result in matching bases?
questions.explanation: |Φ+⟩ = (|00⟩ + |11⟩)/√2 gives perfect correlation: both get 0 or both get 1 when measuring in the same basis. The |Ψ⟩ states give anti-correlation (opposite results), which also works but requires Bob to flip his bit.

questions.question: Why can the entanglement source be untrusted in entanglement-based QKD?

options.text: The source is too simple to manipulate
Bell test detects any non-quantum source
Photons are measured before reaching Eve

questions.explanation: If the source is producing classical correlations or has hidden variables, it cannot produce Bell inequality violations. The Bell test directly verifies quantum behavior regardless of who controls the source. Even Eve running the source cannot fake quantum correlations.

questions.question: E91 was proposed before BB84 as the first QKD protocol.
questions.explanation: False. BB84 was proposed in 1984, while E91 was proposed by Artur Ekert in 1991. BB84 is a prepare-and-measure protocol, while E91 introduced the entanglement-based approach with Bell inequality testing.
